meta.title: 14.3 Conway's Law and Team Topology
meta.description: How organizational structure shapes system architecture. Stream-aligned, platform, enabling, and complicated-subsystem teams.

blocks.prompt: A company has three teams: frontend, backend, and database. They are building a new product. What architecture will they most likely produce, and why?

blocks.content:
## Conway's Law

> "Any organization that designs a system will produce a design whose structure is a copy of the organization's communication structure." — Melvin Conway, 1967

This is not just an observation — it is a law of nature in software. A company with frontend, backend, and database teams will produce a three-tier architecture. A company with teams organized by business domain will produce a service-oriented architecture.

**The Inverse Conway Maneuver:** If you want a specific architecture, organize your teams to match it. Want microservices? Create small, autonomous teams aligned to business capabilities. Want a monolith? Have everyone on one team.

## Team Topologies

Matthew Skelton and Manuel Pais identified four fundamental team types that produce effective software architectures:

### 1. Stream-Aligned Teams
The primary team type. Aligned to a single stream of business value (a product, a feature set, a user journey). They own their services end-to-end: design, build, deploy, operate.

**Characteristics:**
- Cross-functional (frontend, backend, data, QA)
- Can deliver value independently without waiting for other teams
- Own their deployment pipeline and on-call rotation
- Size: 5-9 people (two-pizza teams)

**Example:** A "Checkout Team" that owns the entire checkout experience — UI, API, payment integration, order database.

### 2. Platform Teams
Provide internal services that reduce cognitive load for stream-aligned teams. They build self-service tools and infrastructure so stream-aligned teams do not have to.

**What they provide:**
- CI/CD pipelines and deployment tooling
- Monitoring, logging, and alerting infrastructure
- Database provisioning and management
- Service mesh, API gateways, authentication

**Key principle:** Platform teams treat stream-aligned teams as their customers. The platform should be self-service — if a stream-aligned team needs to file a ticket and wait, the platform has failed.

### 3. Enabling Teams
Specialists who help stream-aligned teams adopt new technologies or practices. They do not build features — they build capabilities.

**Examples:**
- A team that helps other teams adopt Kubernetes
- Security specialists who embed with teams to improve their security practices
- Data engineering coaches who help teams build their own data pipelines

**Key principle:** Enabling teams work themselves out of a job. They succeed when stream-aligned teams no longer need them for that capability.

### 4. Complicated-Subsystem Teams
Own components that require deep specialist knowledge (video codec, ML model, real-time trading engine). These exist because the cognitive load is too high for a stream-aligned team to own alongside their business domain.

## Interaction Modes

Teams interact in three ways:

- **Collaboration:** Two teams work closely together for a defined period (discovery phase, new integration). High bandwidth but expensive — use sparingly.
- **X-as-a-Service:** One team provides a service that another consumes via a clear API or contract. Low overhead, scales well.
- **Facilitating:** An enabling team coaches a stream-aligned team. Temporary by nature.

## Cognitive Load

The central concept behind team topologies. Every team has a limited capacity for complexity. Three types:

- **Intrinsic:** The complexity of the business domain itself.
- **Extraneous:** The complexity of tools, processes, and infrastructure (should be minimized by platform teams).
- **Germane:** The complexity of learning and improving (supported by enabling teams).

When a team's cognitive load exceeds capacity, quality drops, delivery slows, and burnout increases. The solution is not more people — it is reducing extraneous load and splitting domains.

blocks.question: A company reorganizes from technology-layer teams (frontend, backend, database, infrastructure) to business-domain teams (payments, inventory, user accounts). What architectural changes will naturally follow, and what transitional challenges should they expect?
blocks.answer:
**Architectural changes:** The three-tier architecture will naturally evolve toward service-oriented or microservices architecture. Each domain team will build and own their full stack — their own APIs, databases, and UIs. Services will align to business boundaries (payments service, inventory service, accounts service). Teams will define clear API contracts between their services instead of sharing databases.

**Transitional challenges:** (1) Shared database decomposition — the old shared database must be split, and this is the hardest part. Teams must agree on data ownership boundaries. (2) Skill gaps — former backend-only developers now need to learn some frontend, and vice versa. Enabling teams can help here. (3) Duplicated effort — each team may build similar infrastructure (logging, auth). A platform team should form to provide shared tooling. (4) Cross-cutting features — some features span multiple domains (a promotion that affects payments AND inventory). Teams need clear interaction protocols. (5) Political resistance — people identify with their technical specialty and may resist reorganization.

blocks.content: Conway's Law means your architecture will mirror your org chart whether you plan for it or not. Use the Inverse Conway Maneuver — organize teams to match your desired architecture. The four team types (stream-aligned, platform, enabling, complicated-subsystem) provide a framework for sustainable software delivery. Minimize extraneous cognitive load so teams can focus on their business domain.

blocks.prompt: Understanding team structure helps you make better decisions. Module 14.4 covers the formal frameworks for technology decision-making — ADRs, build vs. buy, and evaluation criteria.
